segments.s757-g9: نهانی is an adverb meaning 'secretly' and modifies the verb گفت. بدو is the indirect object pronoun 'to him/to her' placed before the verb (classical Persian allows object pronouns before the verb). گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (verb stem 'گو'), and مهتر پسر is the agent/nominative ('the elder/chief, O son') placed after the verb as a topical or vocative phrase — word order is flexible in poetic Persian.
segments.s757-g10: که introduces reported content ('that'); اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; به + گیتی تبیین a locative use meaning 'in the world' (به + گیتی, here 'به' functions as a locative), توی is a prepositional/adverbial 'in/among', and تاجور is the predicate noun 'merchant' describing تو — together they form the reported clause 'that now in the world you are a merchant.'
segments.s757-g11: مباش is the prohibitive imperative 'do not be' (negative command). ایمن is an adjectival/stative complement 'safe' (i.e., 'do not be secure'). و links clauses ('and/then'); گنج را marks گنج as a definite direct object with را, and چاره کن is an imperative 'make a provision/arrange a remedy' where کن is the imperative 'do.'
segments.s757-g12: جهان بان شدی is a verbal phrase: جهان‑بان ('keeper of the world') is a compound agent noun and شدی is the past/Perfective 'you became' (شدن). کار یکباره کن is an imperative sequence: کار 'affair/deed' + یکباره 'at once' + کن 'do' — an instruction to carry out the task immediately. Overall this segment states the addressee's new role and gives the order to act promptly.
segments.s757-g13: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'if/when' introducing a conditional clause (here best read 'if'). از indicates origin 'from/out of'; تخمه شهریاران is a genitive construction 'the stock/offspring of kings' (تخمه = offspring, شهریاران = of kings). کسی is the indefinite 'someone' which is the subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s757-g14: بیاید is a jussive/subjunctive/potential form 'may come' or 'should come' matching the conditional in چو. نمانی is the negative-future/optative of ماندن 'you will not remain' (second person), تو is the pronoun 'you' (emphatic), ایدر 'here', and بسی is an adverb meaning 'long/much' ('not remain here long'). Together they give the consequence clause of the condition.
segments.s757-g15: وزان پس چنین is a temporal/linking phrase 'and from that/then thus' that introduces reported speech. گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing what was said (here listed sense entries did not include the verb reading, so marked below). کهتر پسر is the agent/subject 'the younger/inferior son' (کهتر used as 'younger' or 'lesser'), placed after گفت in the poetic word order.
segments.s757-g16: This segment repeats the reported clause: که introduces the content ('that'), اکنون 'now', به گیتی توی 'in the world' (به + گیتی + توی as locative expression), and تاجور 'merchant' as the predicate referring to تو. The structure mirrors the earlier reported statement but is framed as what the younger son said.
segments.s757-g97: همی is a habitual/continuous adverbial particle meaning ‘continually’ or ‘kept on’. این … آن … از … بستد … و آن ازین sets up reciprocal action: ‘this (این) from that (از آن) بستد’ and ‘and that (آن) from this (از این)’. بستد is a past tense verb (classical/poetic past) meaning ‘closed/seized/struck’ here; word order is V final with particles framing the two directions of action.
segments.s757-g98: یکی یافت is a short clause: یکی ‘one (of them)’ + یافت (past of یافتن) ‘found/received’. نفرین and آفرین are the two contrasting outcomes coordinated without an overt conjunction: ‘one found/received نفرین (a curse), دگر آفرین (another [received] praise)’, where دگر modifies آفرین as ‘another’. آفرین here means ‘praise/acclamation’, contrasting with نفرین.
segments.s757-g99: پراگنده گشت is a nominative-predicate phrase: پراگنده (adjective/adverb ‘scattered’) + گشت (past of گشتن ‘became’) = ‘became scattered’. آن سپاه بزرگ is the subject (definite: that great army/host) following the predicate in poetic word order.
segments.s757-g100: چو … که introduces a simile/clause: چومیشان functions as ‘چو + میشان’ (’like/as their flocks’) and is followed by بددل ‘fickle/unsteady’ describing them. که بینند گرگ is a subordinate clause with که linking the condition/occurrence (‘when/that they see a wolf’) and بینند (present-subjunctive/optative form used in poetry for ‘they see’). The whole segment means ‘like fickle flocks who, when they see a wolf…’.
segments.s757-g101: فراوان modifies the verb phrase bماندند: فراوان بماندند = ‘they remained in great number/many remained’. بی شهریار is a prepositional/nominal phrase with بی ‘without’ + شهریار ‘king’, giving the circumstance: ‘without a king’. Word order (adverb – verb – circumstance) is normal in poetry.
segments.s757-g102: نیامد کسی is a negative existential construction: نیامد (past negative ‘did not come’) + کسی (here interpreted as ‘anyone/no one’), so ‘no one came’. تاج را خواستار is an object phrase: تاج (crown) + را (definite object marker) + خواستار (requesting/desirous), i.e. ‘asking/requesting the crown’. The clause reads ‘no one came requesting the crown.’
segments.s757-g103: بجستند is a past verb meaning ‘they sought/searched for’ (classical past). فرزند شاهان is the object phrase ‘sons/descendants of kings’ (genitive), and بسی here is an adverb meaning ‘many/much’ (classical use: ‘very/much’). Together: ‘they sought many sons of kings.’
segments.s757-g104: ندیدند is the past negative ‘they did not see’. زان (from آن) introduces ‘of those/from that [group]’ and نامداران (‘renowned men’) is the noun being referred to; کسی after a negative verb functions as ‘anyone/no one’ — here the sense is ‘they saw none of those renowned men.’ Word order is V – adjunct – object – indefinite pronoun in negative scope.
segments.s757-g41: This noun phrase places the time/setting word شب ('night') before its qualifier تیره ('dark/ominous'), then the adverb همواره ('always/ever') and finally گردان ('troop/contingent'). Word order is typical Persian: modifiers follow the head noun or are inserted for emphasis; همواره here marks continuous or repeated occurrence. گردان بدی is a genitive-like pairing meaning 'a troop/host of evil' with بدی as the qualifying noun.
segments.s757-g42: This clause gives two contrasting places introduced by the preposition به: 'به پالیزها‌گر' (to/at the orchards/gardens) and 'به میدان' (to the field/arena). پالیزها‌گر is an agentive/occupational noun ('gardener/field-tiller') or, read as a place-phrase, indicates orchards/fields; here it functions to mark one kind of place where evil moves. The final بدی repeats/echoes 'evil' as the object/qualifier of the preceding locative phrases.
segments.s757-g43: نماندش is a compact past form meaning 'did not remain' (here with the clitic -ش referring to 'him/it'). The locative phrase به ایران follows to give the place, and یکی دوستدار ('one/supporter') is the noun phrase that is the logical object of نماندش: 'not one supporter remained in Iran.' Note the indefinite یکی used like 'not a single.'
segments.s757-g44: شکست is the noun 'defeat' placed at the start of the clause as subject; اندر آمد is a classical/periphrastic verbal phrase meaning 'came in' or 'came to be,' and به آموزگار literally 'to/into (the role of) teacher.' Together the line personifies defeat: 'defeat came in as a teacher' (i.e., defeat became the teacher).
segments.s757-g45: فرایین is an epithet meaning 'exalted/superior' placed before همان ('that/same') for emphasis; ناجوانمرد is a descriptive predicate 'cowardly/ungentlemanly.' The verb گشت ('became') is the past/resultative copula placed at the end, so the clause reads 'that exalted one became dishonourable/cowardly.'
segments.s757-g46: This sequence lists the traits he acquired: the first phrase is textually unusual (ابی داد) and in classical/poetic texts may represent a variant spelling of بی‌داد ('injustice') or be an epithet; because the exact reading is uncertain the first word's sense is unclear here. داد ('justice') functions as the core noun; then و بی‌بخشش ('and merciless') is a coordinated adjective with the negative prefix بی-; finally و خرد گشت ('and became small of-mind') uses خرد ('wisdom' or 'sense') with گشت ('became') to indicate loss or diminishment of wisdom. Overall the structure is a string of predicates joined by و with a final verb indicating the change.
segments.s757-g47: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/always' and often appears in epic poetry. زر ('gold') is the direct object; the preposition بر before چشم places 'on the eye(s)'; the repeated بر before the verb links the object to the action. دوختی is the 2nd-person singular past tense 'you sewed/stitched,' so the clause literally reads 'you always sewed gold upon the eyes' (a metaphor for worldly blindness/avarice).
segments.s757-g48: جهان را is a definite object with the accusative/topic marker را ('the world'); به دینار uses the preposition به to indicate the price/means ('for a dinar' or 'for money'); بفروختی is the 2nd-person singular past of فروختن ('you sold'). The clause is a straightforward accusative clause: 'you sold the world for a dinar.'
segments.s757-g65: This clause uses the impersonal/habitual idiom یاد همی‌آید (“it comes to mind / it occurs to one”). نه کس را places ‘no one’ in an emphatic/ topical role with را after it, so the whole phrase means ‘to no one does it occur’ or more naturally ‘no one would think’. از رشک is an ablative phrase ‘from/out of envy’ modifying یاد همی‌آید.
segments.s757-g66: که introduces the clause giving the content of what does not occur to anyone: ‘that you would give your heart…’. پردازدی is an archaic 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive form meaning ‘you would give/offer’ (here ‘give one’s heart’). دل is the direct object ‘heart’, and بدین بد نژاد is a prepositional/attributive phrase ‘to this ill‑born (one)’: ب (= به) = ‘to’, بد = ‘bad/ill’, نژاد = ‘of lineage/lineage’ functioning adjectivally.
segments.s757-g67: بدیشان (به + ایشان) = ‘to them’; چنین = ‘thus/so’. گفت here is the past verbal idea ‘said’ (the provided sense list treats گفت nominally, but in context it functions as the verb ‘said’). شهران گراز is a proper name (Shahrān‑Gorāz), with شهران and گراز together forming the speaker’s name.
segments.s757-g68: که introduces the content clause: این کار ایرانیان شد دراز = ‘this affair/work of the Iranians became long’. این کار = ‘this affair/ business’, ایرانیان = subject ‘the Iranians’, شد is the past of شدن ‘became’ and دراز = ‘long’ (i.e. ‘grew protracted’). Word order is typical SOV for classical Persian: [this affair of the Iranians] [became] [long].
segments.s757-g69: گر = ‘if’. ایدون (a classical/poetic form, here temporalizing) means roughly ‘up to now/at this point’ or ‘if now’; in the provided list it is listed as ‘come/appear’ but functions adverbially. که introduces the conditional content. بر من = ‘against me/toward me’ and نسازید is a negative/subjunctive/imperative form of سازیدن/ساختن (‘do not make/you do not reconcile’); بد here qualifies the manner as ‘badly’ or ‘cruelly’ (in context it signals doing something harsh).
segments.s757-g70: کنید is an imperative/plural present ‘do (you)’, continuing the address. آنک = ‘that which/what’, introducing the content of the imperative. از داد و گردی links two abstract standards: از + داد = ‘according to justice’ (داد = ‘justice’) and گردی here is a classical noun (in the poem’s idiom) meaning a customary/established thing (the provided senses include ‘dust/wandering’, but here it participates in the formula ‘داد و گردی’ meaning ‘justice and custom’). سزد is the impersonal present ‘it befits/is fitting’. Together: ‘do that which is fitting by justice and custom.’
segments.s757-g71: هم‌اکنون = ‘right now/at once’. به نیروی is an instrumental phrase ‘by the power (of…)’; note that به here functions as an instrumental ‘by/through’. یزدان پاک = ‘pure God’ (پاک modifies یزدان). The whole segment sets an immediate, divinely empowered action.
segments.s757-g72: This clause states the speaker’s action. مر is an enclitic/pronominal form meaning ‘me/to me’ or marking the speaker’s role in older usage; here it frames the verb as the speaker’s action. او را = ‘him’ (object with را). ز (poetic = از) + باره = ‘from (his) burden/seat/mount’ (contextually ‘off his horse / from his position’), در آرم is a 1st‑person present/future intention ‘I will put/lay/bring (into)’, and به خاک = ‘to the earth’ (i.e. ‘to the ground’). Together: ‘I will throw/bring him down to the ground.’
segments.s757-g57: گفت is a past-tense verb 'said' with the indirect object marked by به + ایرانیان 'to the Iranians'. کای is a vocative 'O' addressing مهتران ('chiefs, great ones'); the vocative follows the reporting verb here (poetic word order).
segments.s757-g58: شد is the past 'became'. این روزگار is the subject 'this time/age'. فرایین گران is a two-word adjectival phrase where فرایین is an intensifier 'exalted/very' modifying گران 'heavy/grave'; verb-before-subject order is a poetic inversion.
segments.s757-g59: همی‌دارد is a present/habitual form 'holds/keeps' or 'treats'. او is the third-person subject 'he'. مهتران را (را marks a definite object) is 'the chiefs' as the object, and سبک is a predicative complement meaning 'light/insubstantial' (i.e. 'he treats them as of little weight').
segments.s757-g60: چرا ('why') introduces a question; شد is past 'became'; چنین 'so' modifies the following predicate; مغز و دلتان 'mind (intellect) and your heart'—دلتان is the possessed form 'your heart(s)'; تنگ is the predicate adjective 'narrow/tight' (i.e. closed-minded/emotionally constrained).
segments.s757-g61: همه دیده‌ها 'all eyes' is the subject. ز (poetic for 'از') + و here reads as the prepositional/participle cluster meaning 'because of him' (زو = از او), شده 'have become', and پر سرشک 'full of teardrops' is the adjective phrase (پر = 'full (of)'). The clause means 'all eyes, on his account, have become full of tears.'
segments.s757-g62: جگر here denotes the seat of feeling ('heart/liver' metaphorically). پر ز خون شد = 'became full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', خون = 'blood'), and بباید پزشک is an impersonal necessity 'a physician is needed' or 'one must have a physician' (بباید = 'it is necessary/must').
segments.s757-g63: چنین داد پاسخ مرا = 'thus gave (the) answer to me' (داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer', مرا = 'to me'—here written in two pieces in the word list). او را سپاه at the end identifies whose army/which host is meant or 'regarding him, the army'—classical word order places سپاه ('army/host') finally as the subject. The sequence is a poetic reorder of 'the army gave me such an answer.'
segments.s757-g64: که introduces the content clause 'that …'. چون here functions 'since/as' or 'in that' leading into the negative clause; کس 'anyone/person' is negated by نماند 'did not remain/was not left'; از در پیشگاه is a prepositional phrase 'in/at the presence (of the court)' (از = 'in/at' in poetic usage, در پیشگاه = 'in the presence/at court').
segments.s757-g81: کمان رابه بازو همی‌درکشید: کمان (direct object) + را + به (fused as رابه) marks the action's target: 'the bow toward/against the arm.' همی‑درکشید is an older/poetic imperfective form (همی‑ prefix = iterative/continuous + درکشید from درکشیدن 'to draw/pull'), indicating repeated or ongoing action in the past.
segments.s757-g82: گهی در بروگاه بر سرکشید: گهی is an adverb 'now and then/sometimes.' در بروگاه is a locative phrase ('in/at the gateway'); بر (on) combines with سرکشید (past of سرکشیدن) meaning 'reared up' or 'rose up.' Word order places the temporal adverb first and a locative phrase before the verb, typical in epic narration.
segments.s757-g83: به شورش‌گری تیر بازه ببست: به introduces purpose or circumstance ('for' / 'in the act of'), شورش‌گری is a nominal phrase ('rebellion/assault' or 'rebellious action') functioning adverbially, and تیر بازه is a noun phrase ('the hawk-arrow' / 'hawk attached to an arrow') with ببست (past of بستن) meaning 'he tied/bound' — here 'he fastened/bound the hawk to the arrow' (ببست = bound/tied).
segments.s757-g84: چو شد غرقه پیکانش بگشاد شست: چو شد = 'when it became' (temporal clause). غرقه qualifies پیکانش ('his arrow, drenched/soaked'). بگشاد is past of گشودن with the b- prefix, 'he opened/loosed,' and شست here denotes the thumb/hand used in holding the arrow (thumb = شست), so the clause means 'when his arrow was soaked, he loosened/opened his grip (released with his thumb).' The sequence marks a conditional/temporal trigger and the ensuing action.
segments.s757-g85: بزد تیر ناگاه بر پشت اوی: بزد (past of زدن) is the verb 'struck/shot' and precedes its object تیر (arrow) — a common epic inversion. ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly.' بر پشت اوی is the prepositional phrase 'on/upon his back' with اوی = 'his/him' (old third‑person enclitic).
segments.s757-g86: بیفتاد تازانه از مشت اوی: بیفتاد is past 'it fell.' تازانه is an adverb modifying the fall ('freshly' / 'straightaway/violently' depending on nuance; here 'freshly/straightaway'). از مشت اوی is an ablative phrase 'from his fist/hand' (مشت = fist), specifying source. The clause reports the projectile's immediate falling from the man's grip.
segments.s757-g87: همه تیرتا پر در خون گذشت: همه modifies تیرتا (here 'the whole/entire'), and تیرتا...پر is a phrase meaning 'the arrow as far as the feathers.' در خون گذشت is a verbal phrase 'passed through in blood' (گذشت = passed/pierced through). Word order places the extent ('to the feathers') before the locative/result phrase 'in blood.'
segments.s757-g88: سرآهن ازناف بیرون گذشت: سرآهن ('iron-headed' — the metal head of the weapon) is the subject; از ناف is an ablative prepositional phrase 'from the navel.' بیرون گذشت (بیرون + گذشت) is 'passed out/issued forth.' The sequence describes the iron head exiting through the navel, with simple past narration.
segments.s757-g33: هر آنگه که رفتی به می‌سوی باغ is a temporal subordinate clause: هر آنگه که = “whenever/each time that,” followed by رفتی (2nd person past ‘you went’). به + می‌سوی is the light preposition + present-form verb construction meaning ‘toward/into you were going’ (mi‑suy = ‘go toward’), and باغ is the goal 'garden'. Word order is typical classical/poetic Persian: temporal phrase → past verb → direction phrase.
segments.s757-g34: نبردی جز از شمع عنبر چراغ: نبردی here is the negative past 2nd‑person form (ن + بردی) = ‘you did not take/carry’. جز از introduces the exception 'except (from)', and شمع عنبر چراغ is a nominal phrase 'a candle/lamp of amber' (شمع = candle, عنبر specifying amber/ambergris as material or scent, چراغ = lamp/light). The clause thus states what you did (not) take when going to the garden.
segments.s757-g35: همان تشت زرین و سیمین بدی: همان = 'that/the same'; تشت زرین و سیمین is a compound noun phrase 'a basin (tisht) of gold and of silver' (سیمین is omitted from the provided word list but modifies تشت). بدی in this context is an archaic 2nd‑person past form meaning 'you gave' (from دادن) — a verbal use rather than the noun 'evil'. The phrase states a specific gift the addressee gave.
segments.s757-g36: چو زرین بدی گوهر آگین بدی: چو = 'when/as', introducing a comparative or temporal clause. زرین (golden) and گوهر آگین (literally 'jewel‑bright' or 'jewel‑set, shining') are noun phrases describing what was given. The second بدی again is the archaic past 'you gave'. The structure parallels the previous segment: whenever you gave gold, you also gave gem‑adorned (bright) things.
segments.s757-g37: چو هشتاد در پیش و هشتاد پس begins with چو = 'when'; the numeral phrase (هشتاد ... هشتاد) is split by locative/prepositional elements: در پیش = 'in front / before' and پس = 'behind/after'. The clause sets a scene of numbers positioned before and behind.
segments.s757-g38: پس شمع یاران فریادرس: پس here is a sentence‑initial adverb meaning 'then' or 'thereupon'. شمع یاران is a genitival construction 'the lamp of the companions' and فریادرس is a predicate noun 'succor/rescuer' — together: 'then the companions' lamp was a succor' (i.e. came to their aid). Word order places the temporal adverb first, then the noun phrase and its predicate.
segments.s757-g39: همه شب بدی خوردن آیین اوی is a nominative clause describing habitual action: همه شب = 'all night', بدی خوردن is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to do/commit evil' (خوردن collocates here to mark undergoing or performing the action), آیین = 'custom/practice', and اوی is the archaic possessive/pronoun 'his'. Grammatically it means 'It was his custom to do evil all night.'
segments.s757-g40: دل مهتران پرشد ازکین اوی: دل مهتران = 'the hearts of the chiefs/great ones' (مهتران = plural of 'مهتر', 'leading nobles'); پرشد is a past passive/ intransitive 'were filled' or 'became full', از کین اوی = 'with hatred of him' (اوی = 'his/him'). Word order is subject (possessive noun phrase) → verb → ablative phrase expressing cause.
segments.s757-g49: همی‌ریخت is a poetic imperfect/past-continuous form (همی- prefix + ریخت) meaning ‘was pouring/kept pouring’. خون سر بی‌گناه is a compressed genitive chain without ezafe typical of poetry (literally خونِ سرِ بی‌گناه) = ‘the blood of the innocent (heads)’. Word order places the verb before its (poetic) object.
segments.s757-g50: از آن پس is a temporal phrase ‘after that’ (از = from). برآشفت is past simple with the intensive prefix بر- meaning ‘became angry/was roused’. به روی سپاه is an adverbial phrase ‘against/upon the face of the host/army’ (به + روی + سپاه) indicating the target of the anger.
segments.s757-g51: به دشنام is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘with curses’ (به used instrumentally = by/with). لبها is the direct object ‘their lips’ and بیاراستند is the past plural verb ‘they adorned/arrayed’ (poetic past form = they put). The literal sense: ‘they adorned their lips with curses.’
segments.s757-g52: جهانی همه is an emphatic subject phrase ‘the whole world / everyone’. مرگ او is the object ‘his death’ (ezafe/را omitted in poetry) and خواستند is past plural ‘they wished/desired’. Word order is subject–object–verb, a normal Persian past construction with poetic inversions.
segments.s757-g53: This is an asyndetic poetic list of nominative items: شب تیره (‘dark night’), هرمزد (a personal name here), شهران (‘townsmen/citizens’), گراز (a brute/ferocious one). The nouns are listed without linking particles or ezafe, a common classical poetic device.
segments.s757-g54: سخنها (‘words/things said’) is the plural subject/complement, همی‌گفت is the past-continuous ‘kept saying’, چندان is an adverb ‘to such an extent / many (so much)’, and به راز is a prepositional phrase ‘in secret’ (به used as ‘in’ here). Together: ‘kept saying so many things in secret.’
segments.s757-g55: گزیده سواری is a noun phrase ‘a chosen/selected rider (horseman)’ with adjective before noun, common in poetic diction. ز شهر صطخر uses the poetic short preposition ز (= از) ‘from’ + place name صطخر (Istakhr): ‘from the city of Istakhr.’
segments.s757-g56: که introduces a relative clause ‘who/that’. آن مهتران را uses آن + مهتران with را marking them as the (experiencing) group, بدو is an indirect-object/adverbial ‘to/for him’, بود is past ‘was’, and فخر is the predicate ‘pride’. The clause literally reads ‘that for those chiefs was pride’ = ‘who was the pride of those chiefs.’
segments.s757-g1: This is a temporal clause headed by چو (here 'when') following the adjective/epithet فرایین 'the exalted one'. The verb برنهاد is past tense (classical past) 'placed/raised on'; word order is subject (فرایین) + temporal marker (چو) + object (تاج کیان) + verb, a common poetic order where the action (placing the crown) closes the clause.
segments.s757-g2: همی‌گفت is an iterative imperfect/continuous past ('kept saying'); چیزی که آمدش یاد is a relative clause 'that which came to his memory/mind'. آمدش is a fused verb+object (آمد + ـش = 'came to him'), and یاد is used as 'thought/memory' here. The whole segment reports repeated speech following the prior temporal clause.
segments.s757-g3: همی‌گفت again marks repeated utterance. شاهی کنم is an infinitive/verb construction where کردن functions as the verbal auxiliary after شاهی (kingship) to mean 'to be king' or 'to rule' — here in first-person (کنم) as reported speech. یک زمان is a time-duration ('for a while'/'for a time') modifying the verb.
segments.s757-g4: نشینم is 1st-person subjunctive/present used in reported speech to express 'I will sit' or 'let me sit'. برین is the preposition بر + archaic demonstrative/infix ین (here 'on this'), so برین تخت = 'on this throne'. The final بر شادمان attaches بر + adjective 'شادمان' ('joyful') to indicate the speaker's joyful state while sitting; the repeated بر is a poetic placement of the adposition.
segments.s757-g5: The phrase begins with the comparative head به از meaning 'better than'. بندگی is the noun 'servitude/serving' as the thing being compared. توختن is an archaic/poetic infinitive here; شست and سال follow as a time/measure phrase ('worn/washed hand' + 'year(s)') — in classical poetry such clustered nouns form a durational or descriptive phrase modifying the implied action ('toil/wear out one's hands for years').
segments.s757-g6: This clause uses two verb phrases in compact poetic past/participial forms. برآورده here has the sense of having brought/borne forth and takes رنج ('suffering') as its object; و links to the following phrase. فرو برده is a compound where فرو (down) + برده (past participle 'taken/put') means 'has put down' or 'let fall' and applies to یال ('mane'), a metaphor for pride. The verbs are perfective/resultant participles describing endured hardship and humbled bearing.
segments.s757-g7: پس از من 'after me' sets a temporal sequence; پسر is the subject 'my son'. بر نشیند is a subjunctive/future-like third-person form meaning 'will sit/take the seat' (classical narrative uses present/subjunctive to indicate future action). بگاه ('at the time'/'at the appointed moment') specifies when he will take the seat.
segments.s757-g8: نهد is a present/subjunctive/intentional verb form 'he will place' (or 'sets') and takes بر سر 'on the head' as the locative prepositional phrase; آن خسروانی کلاه is the direct object 'that royal hat/crown'. Word order is verb + locative + object in the poetic register, with نهد governing the action of placing the royal headgear.
segments.s757-g73: چنین (thus) is an adverb placed before the verb to mark the manner. یافت is the past tense, 3rd-person singular of یافتن/یابیدن meaning 'found/obtained' (here 'received'). پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (classical form of از) and ایرانیان is the source noun 'the Iranians'. Word order: adverb + verb + object + source phrase.
segments.s757-g74: که introduces the reported/content clause ('that' / marking quoted wish). بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'upon you' (بر = on/upon, تو = you). مبادا is an optative/negative-jussive particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'lest'. The following که + آید زیان is a subordinate clause after مبادا: the second که is a connective 'that' and آید is present-subjunctive/habitual of آمدن used here as 'come/happen'; زیان = 'harm/detriment'. Together: 'that no harm should come upon you.'
segments.s757-g75: همه لشکر is a noun phrase 'the whole army/host' (همه modifies لشکر). امروز is a temporal adverb 'today' placed after the noun. یار is predicate 'companion/ally' and توایم is an archaic contracted copula meaning 'I am yours' / 'I am with you' — here the phrase functions idiomatically to assert alliance/support. In poetry the copula/to-be form may follow the predicate.
segments.s757-g76: گرت is the conditional particle 'if' (classical/poetic form of اگر). زین literally 'from this' but here names the saddle/kit (زین = 'saddle/trappings'); بد is the adjective 'bad/ill' modifying an implied misfortune, and آید is present/subjunctive of آمدن used for potential/hypothetical 'should come/happen'. حصار is 'rampart/fortress/bulwark' and توایم again is the first-person copula 'I am yours' — together: 'if some ill comes from this saddle, I will be your bulwark.' Note classical sequence: conditional + آید (present) + present copula conveys future/hypothetical result.
segments.s757-g77: چو is the conjunction 'when' (classical usage). بشنید is the simple past, 3rd-person singular of شنیدن 'he heard'. ز ایشان is the source phrase 'from them'. The second ز governs ترک + ش: ترک (here 'Turk') with the enclitic ش ('his/their') yields ترکش meaning 'of the Turk / the Turk's (— )'. نخست is the adverb 'first/at first'. The sequence gives temporal framing: 'When he heard that from them, from the Turk's ... first ...'.
segments.s757-g78: یکی is the indefinite numeral 'one/a certain'. تیر and پیکان are both words for 'arrow' (poetic pleonasm); پولاد is an attributive noun 'steel' (i.e. steel-headed). بجست is the past tense of جستن used with motion sense 'leapt/flew forth'. Word order: numeral + noun + modifiers + verb yields 'one steel arrow leapt forth.'
segments.s757-g79: برانگیخت is a past-tense verb 'he roused/raised' (from برانگیختن). از جای is the prepositional phrase 'from the place' (از = from, جای = place/position). اسپ سیاه is the noun + adjective 'black steed' (اسپ = horse, سیاه = black). Word order is verb + source phrase + object phrase: 'he roused the black steed from its place.'
segments.s757-g80: همی‌داشت is a past-continuous/formal compound ('was holding' / 'kept') used here to mean 'kept' or 'maintained'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. نگاه is the noun 'gaze/look' which functions as the direct object of داشت. او را is the normal object phrase 'him' (را marks definite object). مر is an enclitic/archaic element ('me' / 'to me')—in classical lines such enclitics often indicate the poet-speaker's perspective or dative sense; syntactically the clause thus says roughly 'the army kept its gaze on him' (with مر adding a personal margin/attestation).
segments.s757-g25: ورا is the clitic object 'to him' placed before the predicate for emphasis. خوش نیامد is a fixed past construction (خوش + نیامد) meaning 'was not pleasing' or 'did not please' (نیامد here is not literal 'come' but idiomatic). بدین سان سخن is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'speech in this manner' (بدین سان = 'in this way') that specifies what did not please him.
segments.s757-g26: به + recipient (مهتر پسر) marks the indirect object 'to the elder son' before the verb گفت. Here گفت functions as the reporting verb introducing quoted or imperative speech. خامی مکن is a negative imperative: خامی (naiveté/inexperience) is the substantive notion and مکن is the negative imperative of کردن ('do not'); together it means 'do not be naive/foolish.'
segments.s757-g27: عرض (here 'a petition/presentation') is the direct object, marked by را and placed before the verb (common narrative word order). به دیوان شاهی is the prepositional phrase 'before/to the royal divan/court.' نشاند is an archaic/transitive past (here meaning 'placed/caused to be set' or 'presented') rather than the agricultural sense; it functions as the main verb 'presented.'
segments.s757-g28: سپه را is the object 'the army' marked by را. سراسر here means 'entire/throughout' and qualifies that the whole army is meant. به درگاه خواند: به درگاه = 'to the court/presence' and خواند is past of خواندن used transitively in the sense 'to call/summon' (not 'to read'), so the clause means 'he summoned the entire army to the court.'
segments.s757-g29: شب تیره تا روز is a temporal adverbial 'from the dark night until the day' (تا = 'until'). دینار داد is a simple past clause 'gave dinars' with داد as the past tense of دادن ('gave'), so the whole segment describes continuous or repeated giving over that time span.
segments.s757-g30: بسی here functions adverbially as 'many/a great deal.' خلعت is the direct object 'robes of honour' and ناسزاوار modifies it meaning 'undeserved/unseemly' (i.e., not merited). داد is the past 'gave/ bestowed.' The sequence describes generous bestowal of robes that were not fittingly earned.
segments.s757-g31: به دو هفته is a temporal phrase 'within two weeks' (or 'in two weeks'). از گنج شاه اردشیر is a partitive/genitive phrase 'from the treasure of King Ardashir' (از + noun indicating source). The whole prepositional phrase sets the time and source for what follows.
segments.s757-g32: نماند is the past intransitive 'remained' used here in the sense 'there was left' (with a negative implication in context: nothing remained). از بهایی = 'of value/price' (partitive), qualifying what might remain. یکی پر تیر is a noun phrase 'one feather of an arrow' (یکی = 'one', پر تیر = 'feather of an arrow' with genitive relationship); together the clause says essentially that only one trivial item (an arrow feather) of value remained.
segments.s757-g17: سزاوار شاهی is a predicative phrase meaning “fit/appropriate for kingship” placed before its subjects (inverted order common in classical Persian). سپاهست is an older/poetic fusion of سپاه + است and names one of the fitting things; the coordinating و links سپاه(ست) and گنج (i.e. “an army and treasure”).
segments.s757-g18: چو ... باشی introduces a conditional/clausal clause (“if/when you are ...”). با گنج is an ablative/associate phrase “with/possessing treasure.” باشی is the 2nd-person singular (subjunctive/optative/presence) of بودن used here to mean “you are / you have” (classical style). نمانی is the negative-future/result clause meaning “you will not remain”; به رنج = “in hardship.”
segments.s757-g19: فریدون is the proper name (head of the noun phrase). که introduces a relative clause ‘whose/who’. بد here is an epithet attached before the name (classical placement) meaning ‘ill/evil/unfortunate’. آبتینش contains the name آبتین plus the possessive enclitic ‑ش “his/its” (i.e. ‘his Abtin’), and پدر follows to state the relation: ‘whose father was Abtin.’
segments.s757-g20: مر is an older oblique form equivalent to مرا/مَن (’to me’ / ‘me’) used as an indirect object; او را is the pronoun 'him' marked with را (definite object/emphatic marker). که begins a subordinate clause ‘that/who’; پیش او = ‘before/in the presence of him’ (پیش = before/in front of); تاجور = ‘merchant’. The whole fragment functions as reported/relative content: ‘(he) to me, that (he) before him was a merchant.’
segments.s757-g21: جهان را is the direct object/topic ('the world'), with را marking definiteness/emphasis. بسه here reads as classical/poetic compound for به سه (‘to/for three’), introducing the number of sons; پور = 'son' (a common name-element and noun); فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' following پور; داد is the past-tense verb 'gave.' The clause therefore states that he 'gave the world three fortunate sons.'
segments.s757-g22: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. اندر جهان = 'in the world'; او is the subject pronoun 'he'. بد here is an epithet/adverbial qualifer (classical usage is compressed and can mean ‘well/ill/so’) and combines with از داد شاد; از = 'because of' / 'from'; داد can be read as either 'giving' or 'justice' depending on context; شاد = 'happy'/'glad'. The phrase explains that in the world he was happy/prosperous because of his giving/just deeds.
segments.s757-g23: به مرد و به گنج is a doubled prepositional phrase 'by/for men and by/for treasure' indicating the proper supports of the world or rulership. این جهان را is the object/topic 'this world' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). بدار here is an imperative/hortative form meaning 'keep/hold' (classical verbal usage), commanding to 'hold/entrust the world to men and wealth.'
segments.s757-g24: نزاید is a present/past-form with the negative prefix ن‑ meaning 'is not born / does not come into being' (classical verb زاییدن). ز مادر = 'from the mother' (poetic preposition ز = از). کسی is the indefinite 'anyone' and شهریار is the predicate noun 'sovereign/king.' The clause states the proverb-like idea that simply being born of a mother does not make one a king.
segments.s757-g89: ز + باره is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from that blow/instance' (ز = 'from'); در افتاد is a compound verbal phrase 'fell in/was struck down' where در is part of the phrasal idea and افتاد is the past-tense verb 'fell'; سر is the anatomical subject 'head' and سرنگون is a postpositive adjective 'cast down/overthrown.' Word order is inverted/poetic: prepositional phrase → verb → subject + adjective.
segments.s757-g90: روان here means 'the soul/spirit' and گشت is a past verb meaning 'went/left' (so together 'his soul departed'). زان = 'from that' (referring back to the wound), زخم او = 'his wound' (زخم + possessive او), and جوی خون is a noun phrase 'a stream of blood' (جوی = stream, خون = blood); the clause after the soul-verb describes the blood that issued from the wound.
segments.s757-g91: بپیچید is a past intransitive verb 'it turned/whirled' (here describing motion), و is the coordinating 'and', برزد is an archaic past verb 'fell/struck' (here 'smote'), and یکی باد سرد is the postverbal subject phrase 'a certain/certain one — a cold wind.' Poetic inversion places the verbs before the subject.
segments.s757-g92: به زاری = 'with lamentation' or 'in lamenting' (به as an adverbial/locative particle + زاری 'lamentation'); بران = 'upon that' (pointing to the dust/ground just mentioned); خاک دل پر ز درد strings nouns adjectivally: خاک (earth/dust), دل (heart) modified by پر ز درد = 'full of pain' (پر 'full (of)' + ز 'from/of' + درد 'sorrow/pain'). The clause describes lamenting upon the dust by a heart full of sorrow.
segments.s757-g93: سپه (the host/army) is the subject and تیغها (swords) is the object; بر + کشیدند is the compound verb برکشیدن 'to draw (weapons)' in the past plural 'drew,' and پاک is an adverbial/postpositive modifier here meaning 'completely/cleanly.' Note the V-final/compound-verb structure and that پاک modifies the preceding action.
segments.s757-g94: برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'; شب تیره is the subject phrase 'a dark/ominous night' (adjective after noun), and از دشت خاک is a source phrase 'from the plain's dust' (از = 'from', دشت = 'plain', خاک = 'dust' — 'the dusty plain'). The clause means a dark night rose out of the dusty plain.
segments.s757-g95: همه شب is an adverbial temporal phrase 'all night' (throughout the night); همی is an iterative/emphatic particle 'kept on/continually'; خنجر is the object 'daggers'; and انداختند is the past plural verb 'they threw.' The phrase describes a repeated action over the night.
segments.s757-g96: یکی از دگر is the idiom 'one from (the) other' (= 'one another'); باز نشناختند is the negative past 'did not recognize/know again' — here the combination expresses 'they could no longer recognize one another' (باز with negation often gives the sense 'no longer/anymore').

sentences.s757-s5: The elder quietly said to him, "Now in the world you are a merchant."
sentences.s757-s6: Do not be secure; find a remedy for the treasure; you have become the world's guardian — do the matter at once.
sentences.s757-s7: If someone from the seed of kings should appear, you will not remain here long.
sentences.s757-s8: And afterward the younger son said thus: 'Now in the world you are a merchant.'
sentences.s757-s49: This kept striking at that and that at this; one received a curse, another received praise.
sentences.s757-s50: That great host was scattered, like fickle flocks who, when they see a wolf, become dismayed.
sentences.s757-s51: Many remained without a king; no one came asking for the crown.
sentences.s757-s52: They searched for many sons of kings, but they saw none of those renowned men.
sentences.s757-s21: Dark night ever marshalled a troop of evil—whether to the orchards or to the battlefield.
sentences.s757-s22: Not a single supporter remained in Iran; defeat itself came in as a teacher.
sentences.s757-s23: That exalted one became a coward; he grew unjust, merciless, and lost his wisdom.
sentences.s757-s24: You always stitched gold upon your eyes; you sold the world for a dinar.
sentences.s757-s33: No one, out of envy, would think (it would occur to them) that you would give your heart to such an ill‑born person.
sentences.s757-s34: Shahrān‑Gorāz said to them thus: that this affair of the Iranians has become prolonged.
sentences.s757-s35: If, up to now, you will not reconcile with me, then do (to me) what is appropriate by justice and custom.
sentences.s757-s36: At once, by the power of the pure God, I will cast him down to the earth.
sentences.s757-s29: He said to the Iranians, “O chiefs, this age has become exceedingly heavy (grave).”
sentences.s757-s30: He treats the chiefs lightly; why have your minds and hearts become so narrow?
sentences.s757-s31: All eyes, because of him, have become full of tears; the heart is filled with blood — a physician is needed.
sentences.s757-s32: Thus the host/army gave me this reply: 'There is no one left in the royal presence.'
sentences.s757-s41: He kept drawing the bow to his arm; now and then at the gateway he reared up.
sentences.s757-s42: For the sortie he bound a hawk to the arrow; when his arrow became drenched, he loosened his grip.
sentences.s757-s43: An arrow suddenly struck him in the back; it fell straightaway from his fist.
sentences.s757-s44: The whole arrow up to the feathers passed through in blood; the iron head passed out from his navel.
sentences.s757-s17: Whenever you went toward the garden, you carried nothing except a lamp — a candle scented with amber.
sentences.s757-s18: You gave that same golden and silver basin; when you bestowed gold, you bestowed jewels set aglow.
sentences.s757-s19: When eighty stood before and eighty behind, then the companions' lamp came to their aid.
sentences.s757-s20: All night doing evil was his custom; the hearts of the great ones were filled with hatred of him.
sentences.s757-s25: The innocent's blood kept flowing; after that he became enraged against the army.
sentences.s757-s26: They filled their lips with curses; the whole world wished for his death.
sentences.s757-s27: On the dark night, Hormozd, the townsmen and the brute kept saying many things in secret.
sentences.s757-s28: A chosen horseman from the city of Istakhr, who was the pride of those chiefs.
sentences.s757-s1: When the exalted one set the Kayan crown (upon him), he kept saying whatever came into his mind.
sentences.s757-s2: He kept saying, 'I will be king for a time; I will sit on this throne, joyful.'
sentences.s757-s3: Better than servitude (is) to toil with worn hands for years; he has brought suffering upon himself and let his mane fall low.
sentences.s757-s4: After me my son will sit (on the throne); at the appointed time he will place that royal cap upon his head.
sentences.s757-s37: Thus he received an answer from the Iranians: "May no harm come upon you."
sentences.s757-s38: The whole host is your companion today; if ill from this saddle should come, I will be your bulwark.
sentences.s757-s39: When he heard that from them, from the Turk's (quiver) at first one steel-tipped arrow leapt forth.
sentences.s757-s40: He roused the black steed from its place; the army kept its gaze upon him.
sentences.s757-s13: That speech did not please him; he said to the elder son, "Do not be foolish."
sentences.s757-s14: He set the petition before the royal divan and summoned the entire army to the court.
sentences.s757-s15: Night and dark until day he gave dinars; he bestowed many unmerited robes.
sentences.s757-s16: Within two weeks not a thing of value remained from King Ardashir's treasure except an arrow's feather.
sentences.s757-s9: An army and treasure are fitting for kingship; if you have treasure, you will not remain in hardship.
sentences.s757-s10: Fereydun, whose father was Abtin, (he) told me that before him he had been a merchant.
sentences.s757-s11: He gave the world three fortunate sons; in the world he was made happy (or prospered) by his giving/justice.
sentences.s757-s12: Keep this world for men and for treasure; no one is born a sovereign merely from his mother.
sentences.s757-s45: From that blow his head fell, cast down; his soul departed, and from that wound of his a stream of blood flowed.
sentences.s757-s46: A cold wind turned and struck; upon that dust, lamenting, a heart full of pain.
sentences.s757-s47: The host drew their swords cleanly; a dark night rose from the dusty plain.
sentences.s757-s48: All night long they kept throwing daggers; they no longer recognized one another.
